Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 28 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so clicking (39, 36) moved block 2 from (39-40, 36-37) to (38-39, 35-36). Good, so clicking non-pair cells within the block DOES move it.
But clicking (40, 37) - the pair position - did NOT move the block, even though it's within the block's boundary. This confirms that the pair position is "sticky" and clicking it doesn't move the block.
So the rule is: after teleporting, the pair position becomes part of the block, but clicking it doesn't move the block. You need to click a different cell within the block to move it.
This also explains why my batch of 8 clicks on (40, 37) didn't work - (40, 37) is the pair position, and clicking it doesn't move the block.
Now I need to figure out the goal. Let me reconsider.
Current blocks:
1. (29-30, 10-11) - pair was (41, 16), can't teleport anymore
2. (38-39, 35-36) - pair was (40, 37), teleported, need to click non-pair cells to move
3. (55-56, 46-47) - pair was (56, 47), teleported, need to click non-pair cells to move
4. (56-57, 15-16) - pair was (57, 16), teleported, need to click non-pair cells to move
For blocks 3 and 4, the pair positions are (56, 47) and (57, 16), which are the bottom-right corners. So to move them, I need to click the other 3 cells.
Wait, but earlier I was clicking (56, 47) and (57, 16) for blocks 3 and 4, and they moved! Let me check...
Actually, for blocks 3 and 4, I clicked the pair positions BEFORE teleporting. After teleporting, the pair positions became part of the blocks, but I never tried clicking them again to move the blocks.
Hmm, let me think about this. For blocks 3 and 4:
- Block 3: created at (53-54, 48-49), pair is (56, 47). Teleported to (55-56, 46-47). The pair position (56, 47) is the bottom-right of the block.
- Block 4: created at (54-55, 3-14), pair is (57, 16). Teleported to (56-57, 15-16). The pair position (57, 16) is the bottom-right of the block.
So for all teleported blocks, the pair position is the bottom-right corner. Clicking the bottom-right corner doesn't move the block. I need to click a different cell.
Now, let me think about the goal again. I have 4 magenta blocks that need to be moved up-left. The blue object is at (23-31, 29-37). The top-left colored blocks are at rows 1-2.
Let me try moving all 4 blocks up-left and see if they form a pattern or trigger something. But first, let me figure out which cells to click for each block:
- Block 1: (29-30, 10-11). Pair is (41, 16), which is NOT within the block. So any cell within the block works. Click (30, 11) (bottom-right).
- Block 2: (38-39, 35-36). Pair is (40, 37), which is NOT within the block (block is at 38-39, 35-36). Wait, the block was teleported to (39-40, 36-37), and then I clicked (39, 36) to move it to (38-39, 35-36). The pair position (40, 37) is now outside the block. So any cell within the block works. Click (39, 36) (bottom-right).
- Block 3: (55-56, 46-47). Pair is (56, 47), which IS the bottom-right of the block. Click (55, 46) (top-left) to move.
- Block 4: (56-57, 5-16). Pair is (57, 16), which IS the bottom-right of the block. Click (56, 15) (top-left) to move.
Wait, I need to be more careful. Let me re-check the block positions:
- Block 1: (29-30, 10-11). The pair (41, 16) is outside. Click any cell, e.g., (30, 11).
- Block 2: (38-39, 35-36). The pair (40, 37) is outside. Click any cell, e.g., (39, 36).
- Block 3: (55-56, 46-47). The pair (56, 47) is the bottom-right. Click (55, 46) to move.
- Block 4: (56-57, 15-16). The pair (57, 16) is the bottom-right. Click (56, 15) to move.
Let me move all 4 blocks a few times and see what happens. I'll click the safe cells (not the pair positions).
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is to move all 4 magenta blocks to specific positions that form a pattern. Let me look at the initial positions of the sky blue dots:
- (37, 18), (37, 41), (40, 37), (41, 16), (54, 49), (55, 14), (56, 47), (57, 16)
These 8 dots form 4 pairs. The pairs are:
- ((37, 18), (41, 16))
- ((37, 41), (40, 37))
- ((54, 49), (56, 47))
- ((55, 14), (57, 16))
When I click one dot of a pair, it creates a magenta block, and the other dot disappears. The magenta block can then be moved up-left or teleported to the pair position.
Maybe the goal is to move the magenta blocks to the positions of the colored blocks in the top-left. But as I calculated, none of the blocks can reach those positions by moving up-left.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the magenta blocks until they disappear off the board, and the order of disappearance matters. Or maybe the goal is to move them until they overlap with the blue object.
Actually, let me try yet another hypothesis. Maybe the 4 colored blocks in the top-left (S, M, p, Y) represent the 4 magenta blocks, and the goal is to move each magenta block to a position that corresponds to its colored block. The correspondence might be based on the order of creation:
- Block 1 (created first) → S (sky blue) at (1-2, 1-2)
- Block 2 (created second) → M (magenta) at (1-2, 5-6)
- Block 3 (created third) → p (purple) at (1-2, 9-10)
- Block 4 (created fourth) → Y (yellow) at (1-2, 13-14)
For block 1 to reach (1-2, 1-2): c-r = 1-1 = 0. Current c-r = 10-29 = -19. Need to change c-r by +19. Moving up-left doesn't change c-r. Teleporting doesn't change c-r either (the block moves by the same vector as the pair). So block 1 can't reach (1-2, 1-2).
Hmm, this doesn't work either.
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me just move all 4 blocks up-left until they reach row 25 or overlap with the blue object, and see what happens. I'll use the safe cells (not the pair positions).
Let me batch some moves:
- Block 1: click (30, 11) repeatedly
- Block 2: click (39, 36) repeatedly
- Block 3: click (55, 46) repeatedly
- Block 4: click (56, 15) repeatedly
Actually, let me first try moving just one block (block 2) towards the blue object and see if it interacts. Block 2 is at (38-39, 35-36). The blue object is at (23-31, 29-37). To overlap, block 2 needs to reach row 31. That's 7 more moves: (38-7=31, 35-7=28). So after 7 moves, block 2 would be at (31-32, 28-29), which overlaps with the blue object's bottom-left corner.
Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move block 2 towards the blue object
# Block 2 is at (38-39, 35-36)
# Blue object is at (23-31, 29-37)
# Click (39, 36) - the bottom-right, which is NOT the pair position (40, 37)

# Let me move block 2 7 times to reach row 31
moves = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 39-i, 'col': 36-i} for i in range(7)]
# Wait, I need to click within the CURRENT block position each time
# After each move, the block shifts up-left by (1,1)
# So I need to click the new bottom-right each time

# Actually, let me just click the top-left corner each time, which stays relative
# Block at (r, c) to (r+1, c+1). Top-left is (r, c). After move, top-left is (r-1, c-1).
# So I should click (r-1, c-1) each time, or equivalently, click a fixed offset.

# Easier: just click the top-left of the current block position
# Current: (38, 35). After move: (37, 34). Click (37, 34) next time.
# But I can batch them by computing the sequence:
clicks = []
r, c = 38, 35  # top-left of block 2
for _ in range(7):
    clicks.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c})
    r -= 1
    c -= 1

action(clicks)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
m_blocks = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print("After 7 moves of block 2:")
for m in sorted(m_blocks, key=lambda x: (x['boundary'][0][0], x['boundary'][0][1])):
    print(f"  boundary={m['boundary']}")

# Check blue object
blue = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"\nBlue objects: {len(blue)}")
for b in blue:
    print(f"  pixels={b['pixels']}, boundary={b['boundary'][:4]}...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
